title: It's Nix All The Way Down
summary: I glanced at the clock on my bedside table. Three in the morning. My brain was churning—useless ideas, sweet nothings, anything but the urge to fall asleep. Hardly is my annoyance out when a vast image out of spiritus mundi troubles my sight: somewhere in the sands of my consciousness, a shape with penguin body and the head of an apple, a gaze as blank and…
content:
I glanced at the clock on my bedside table. Three in the morning. My brain was churning—useless ideas, sweet nothings, anything but the urge to fall asleep.

Hardly is my annoyance out when a vast image out of spiritus mundi troubles my sight: somewhere in the sands of my consciousness, a shape with penguin body and the head of an apple, a gaze as blank and pitiless as a segfault, is slowly being developed, while all about it reel shadows of POSIX compatibility.

The ceiling appears again; but now I know that two weeks of writer's block were banished to nightmare by an idea.

And what rough code, its hour come round at last, is added to home.nix to be installed?

You have likely heard of Wine, a project that aims to run Windows applications on "several POSIX-compliant operating systems, such as Linux, macOS, & BSD." It works by translating Windows API calls into the equivalent calls for the other operating system. It isn't a perfect solution, but it works pretty well.

What if you took that concept, but with macOS instead of Windows? Enter Darling, a "translation layer that lets you run macOS software on Linux".

$ sudo darling shell
Bootstrapping the container with launchd...

The default interactive shell is now zsh.
To update your account to use zsh, please run `chsh -s /bin/zsh`.
For more details, please visit https://support.apple.com/kb/HT208050.
Darling# uname
Darwin

Of course, keeping in true Ersei fashion, I have to install Nix on it.

Darling# sh <(curl -L https://nixos.org/nix/install)
...
/unpack/nix-2.16.1-x86_64-darwin/install-darwin-multi-user.sh: line 221: xmllint: command not found
diskutil: did not recognize verb "info"; type "diskutil" for a list
...
env: /usr/sbin/dseditgroup: No such file or directory
...

---- oh no! --------------------------------------------------------------------
Oh no, something went wrong. If you can take all the output and open
an issue, we'd love to fix the problem so nobody else has this issue.

:(

We'd love to help if you need it.

You can open an issue at
https://github.com/NixOS/nix/issues/new?labels=installer&template=installer.md

Or get in touch with the community: https://nixos.org/community
Darling#

Honestly, I don't know what I expected.

The Nix installer fails because the directory services commands (for modifying users and groups) is not available. Furthermore, trying to force single-user installation with --no-daemon will not work because the installer forces multi-user mode on macOS. If that weren't bad enough, Nix has some strange defaults that come into play when it is being run as root. From the manual:

build-users-group: …Defaults to nixbld when running as root, empty otherwise.

They all seem like simple enough fixes, though. Now that the installer script has been sufficiently mutilated to allow installing in single-user mode as root, let's go ahead and install Nix!

Darling# mkdir /etc/nix
Darling# echo "build-users-group =" > /etc/nix/nix.conf
Darling# ./install --no-daemon
warning: installing Nix as root is not supported by this script!
performing a single-user installation of Nix...
copying Nix to /nix/store.........................................................
installing 'nix-2.13.4'
building '/nix/store/07rs9myg8yh532ii196qk5xvfmy2wk9c-user-environment.drv'...
error: clearing flags of path '/nix/store/rghh1vgdc9zd044651054b86y2zsz0lh-user-environment/bin': Invalid argument
./install: unable to install Nix into your default profile

…what does clearing flags of path: Invalid mean? At this point, I was feeling a little bit concerned, but it should not be that hard to track down and fix, right?

Time to do some digging. I grepped through the Nix source to find clearing flags of path. It led me to this code block:

#if __APPLE__
    /* Remove flags, in particular UF_IMMUTABLE which would prevent
       the file from being garbage-collected. FIXME: Use
       setattrlist() to remove other attributes as well. */
    if (lchflags(path.c_str(), 0)) {
        if (errno != ENOTSUP)
            throw SysError("clearing flags of path '%1%'", path);
    }
#endif

Okay, so it seems like lchflags is failing to run. I hope that this isn't a herald of predicaments to come! (foreshadowing!)

ABI Incompatibilities Galore!

From the Apple Documentation:

The file whose name is given by path has its flags changed to flags. See chflags(2) for the values of the flags.

Instead of being a system call, lchflags() is emulated using setattrlist(2). Not all file systems support setattrlist(2).

Okay, so what is causing the problem? I don't want to compile Nix from source just to get more debug info (foreshadowing!!), so let's debug another way instead.

After adding set -x to the install script, the broken step is made apparent: running nix-env.

Darling# /nix/store/51sbkakrhiq8lms2lijkbm947yq9s4y2-nix-2.13.4/bin/nix-env -i /nix/store/51sbkakrhiq8lms2lijkbm947yq9s4y2-nix-2.13.4
installing 'nix-2.13.4'
building '/nix/store/07rs9myg8yh532ii196qk5xvfmy2wk9c-user-environment.drv'...
error: clearing flags of path '/nix/store/rghh1vgdc9zd044651054b86y2zsz0lh-user-environment/bin': Invalid argument

Maybe I can reach out to the Darling folks and see if they have a solution? At the time of writing, I have still not gotten a response on their Discord.

I guess it's time to compile a fixed Nix.

A Sisyphean Effort

Luckily for me, Nix has a list of prerequisites available. I installed Brew to download the prerequisites and save me from compiling the dependencies from scratch (foreshadowing!!!).

Darling# /bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"
==> Checking for `sudo` access (which may request your password)...
Don't run this as root!

Thank you, Brew, for saving me.

Although Darling does not yet have support for creating new accounts, I can trick Brew by changing my UID and GID.

Darling# sudo -u 1000 -g 1000 bash
Darling$ /bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"
...
==> You are using macOS 10.15.
==> We (and Apple) do not provide support for this old version.
This installation may not succeed.
After installation, you will encounter build failures with some formulae.
Please create pull requests instead of asking for help on Homebrew's GitHub,
Twitter or any other official channels. You are responsible for resolving any
issues you experience while you are running this old version.
...
Floating point exception: 8 (core dumped)

Not ominous at all! I also learn that Darling is pretending to be macOS 10.15 (Catalina). After waiting an hour for Brew to install and fail (did I mention that Darling is pretty slow?), I get a Floating point exception: 8 (core dumped).

What does that even mean? Maybe I just try again?

Error: Your Xcode (9.2 => /Library/Developer/CommandLineTools) is too outdated.
Please update to Xcode 12.4 (or delete it).
Xcode can be updated from the App Store.

Wonderful. Time to upgrade then. I can grab the latest version from Apple's website and oh my god it needs me to log in with my Apple ID.

The universe was telling me to stop.

I should have stopped.

But success was so close! I just had to keep going.

Of course, installing newer version of xcode-utils is when Darling chose to spontaneously combust and everything stopped working. Nothing darling shutdown can't fix! After installing the newer developer tools (that I found were not ABI compatible with macOS 10.15 surprise), I piped in code from the internet into a very-insecure-program that has root access to my computer again.

Warning: You are using macOS 10.15.
We (and Apple) do not provide support for this old version.
It is expected behaviour that some formulae will fail to build in this old version.
It is expected behaviour that Homebrew will be buggy and slow.
Do not create any issues about this on Homebrew's GitHub repositories.
Do not create any issues even if you think this message is unrelated.
Any opened issues will be immediately closed without response.
Do not ask for help from Homebrew or its maintainers on social media.
You may ask for help in Homebrew's discussions but are unlikely to receive a response.
Try to figure out the problem yourself and submit a fix as a pull request.
We will review it but may or may not accept it.
...
Error: Empty installation
==> Installation successful!

I'm sure it's fine. It did say "Installation successful", so let's keep chugging!

Darling$ brew install automake autoconf autoconf-archive libpthread-stubs pkg-config libtool make jq boost gettext libtool curl intltool
...
==> Running `brew cleanup autoconf`...
==> ./configure --prefix=/usr/local/Cellar/autoconf-archive/2023.02.20
==> make install
Error: Empty installation

Error: Empty installation makes its second (and hopefully last) appearance on this show.

Brew isn't working, despite what Darling's wiki says. If only I had a functional (both ways) package manager to help me out… soon, I promise myself. Soon.

I guess it's time to do what I was trying to avoid the whole time and compile everything from scratch! It won't take that long, right? It'll all be worth it, right? (foreshadowing!!!!)

This time, I decided to not go into this blind and found that the latest version of Xcode that worked with macOS 10.15 was Xcode 11. I also made sure to look at the documentation for compiling the dependencies on macOS. Look at me, reading the docs!

Now for the prerequisites, which consisted of curl-ing the compressed sources, extracting them, running ./configure && make && make install, then repeating the process for all thirty-ish dependencies.

I ran the configure script, installed the dependencies, and repeated and compiled and configured and compiled for over twenty hours. I had to change the C++ standard being used to fix compatibility issues. I had to patch out register hints in strange places. I had to edit configure scripts by hand to patch around checks that caused the scripts to crash with an Abort trap: 6 (core dumped)—it was a check for a compiler instruction, checking for a compression program, or sometimes the configuration scripts would crash just because and work fine if I ran it again.

With days of pain and frustration, etched on my granite face, I had a compiled Nix binary.

Darling$ ./src/nix
dyld: dyld cache load error: shared cache file open() failed
dyld: Symbol not found: __ZTINSt3__14__fs10filesystem16filesystem_errorE
  Referenced from: src/nix
Expected in: /usr/lib/libc++.dylib
 in src/nix
abort_with_payload: reason: dyld cache load error: shared cache file open() failed
Symbol not found: __ZTINSt3__14__fs10filesystem16filesystem_errorE
  Referenced from: src/nix
  Expected in: /usr/lib/libc++.dylib
 in src/nix; code: 4

Would you look at that. None of it mattered in the end anyway.

I tried, I really did. I tried different linking options, I tried different compilers, I tried supplanting different libc++ files, I tried to statically link Nix, none of it worked.

Giving up was tempting, but the sunk cost fallacy had me in its terrible grip. I had no choice but to keep trying.

The NSA Lends Me Some Help

I know that the prebuilt Nix binary at least starts without complaining about missing symbols, unlike the binary I spent four days putting together. Maybe I can just bypass the check?

Let's leave the Darling environment for now and grab a copy of the macOS Nix files. I know that the nix binary is downloaded alongside the installer, so let's poke around in it.

$ strings nix | grep "clearing flags of path"
$

…no results? That's weird. It should be somewhere, right? Are the strings obfuscated for no reason? Maybe I need to put my tax dollars to good use and poke around the file in Ghidra.

Problem is, I don't know how to use Ghidra. No worries, I can figure it out, right?

After stumbling through importing a binary and analyzing it, I have a very busy GUI. Truly, the embodiment of the government in a single Java window.

This is a lot. Maybe I can search for the function that lchflags belongs to? Let's look for canonicalisePathMetaData

I found it! Double clicking on the suspiciously-red token gives me…

Failed to identify external linkage address for <EXTERNAL>​::​nix​::​canonicalisePathMetaData. Unable to perform navigation.

Failed to identify external linkage address? Does that mean that I've been looking in the wrong place the whole time?

Oh, the joys of dynamic linking. Maybe I should look at the other files before I spiral into despair.

$ grep -rn "clearing flags of path"
grep: lib/libnixstore.dylib: binary file matches

There you are! I thank the NSA (just a little) as I curse myself for not looking at the other files too. I open up the library in the slightly-more-familiar Rizin—time to poke around the raw bits and bytes of compiled code!

Because the folks over at Nix release their binaries optimised for production use, they have most symbols stripped. Hopefully it's not optimised to the point where it's impossible to grok what the instructions mean. Due to the optimisation, I can't just tell Rizin to go to the canonicalisePathMetaData function. I can, however, look for lchflags.

[0x00000000]> fl | grep lchflags
0x001d1e90 6 sym.imp.lchflags
0x0021a0a0 8 reloc.lchflags

We don't care about the relocation bits. Let's find out where lchflags is being used:

[0x00000000]> axm 0x001d1e90
0x0012713f CALL
[0x00000000]> s 0x0012713f

Now let's see what's going on and print a couple lines of disassembly.

[0x0012713f]> pd 4
0x0012713f      call  sym.imp.lchflags
0x00127144      test  eax, eax
0x00127146      je    0x127156
0x00127148      call  sym.imp.__error

What's going on here? It's not too complicated. First, lchflags is called, and the result is stored in the register (think variable) eax. Next, we are checking if eax is 0. If it is zero (if lchflags worked), then the program will jump to the address 0x127156. If not, then we will call the error function.

Luckily, lchflags itself is not causing the error. Keeping lchflags is fine! We just need to ignore the result. Luckily, the folks down at Intel have the instruction for us! jmp! The unconditional jump, akin to GOTO of yore.

Why jmp instead of removing the lchflags call entirely? Simple. Binaries are very strict about locations. If you shift the binary (like replacing the lchflags call with the smaller nop (no operation) instruction), then addresses will point to the wrong locations in the binary. I'm sure you can detect and patch around that, but it's a lot more work. Replacing je with jmp will use the same amount of bytes once converted back into machine code.

Let's edit it out1!

[0x00127146]> pd 2
0x00127146      jmp   0x127156
0x00127148      call  sym.imp.__error

Fingers crossed…

$ sudo darling shell
Darling# cd /Volumes/SystemRoot/home/ersei/workspaces/nix-macos/nix-2.13.4-x86_64-darwin
Darling# mkdir /etc/nix
Darling# echo "build-users-group =" > /etc/nix/nix.conf
Darling# ./install --no-daemon

warning: installing Nix as root is not supported by this script!
performing a single-user installation of Nix...
directory /nix does not exist; creating it by running 'mkdir -m 0755 /nix && chown root /nix' using sudo
copying Nix to /nix/store.........................................................
installing 'nix-2.17.0'
building '/nix/store/8wlv1a6xi9mrhv8ggn8r4zhg6yallraw-user-environment.drv'...
installing 'nss-cacert-3.83'
building '/nix/store/lzlkl7y7yssnfmp9k8bl7zn4qpa8g43x-user-environment.drv'...
unpacking channels...

Installation finished!  To ensure that the necessary environment
variables are set, please add the line

  . /Users/root/.nix-profile/etc/profile.d/nix.sh

to your shell profile (e.g. ~/.profile).

Did it… did it just work? You know what I have to do now, right? Install home-manager!

Darling# nix-shell '<home-manager>' -A install
this derivation will be built:
  /nix/store/skd8jyfq47cw73s3h5a1jlgjj1dsmr1l-home-manager.drv
building '/nix/store/skd8jyfq47cw73s3h5a1jlgjj1dsmr1l-home-manager.drv'...
...
error: executing '/nix/store/krj9mlsrl8l544z26680ailir2jxzfqs-bash-5.2-p15/bin/bash': Bad file descriptor
error: builder for '/nix/store/skd8jyfq47cw73s3h5a1jlgjj1dsmr1l-home-manager.drv' failed with exit code 1

Ouch. I really thought that home manager would just work. I guess I never learn.

Disappointment (Again)

Considering that I will never use this system for anything, it's probably fine that I can't get my fancy shell configurations automatically installed.

Maybe it is time to throw off the shackles of the sunk cost fallacy. This system is unstable, lacks features, and the software that won't crash on there will likely not work properly.

Darling# nix-env -i fish
...
Darling# fish
Something called the old LKM API (nr = 4158)
Illegal instruction: 4 (core dumped)

But I really have to know what's causing the issue. Does building anything work in Nix?

Darling$ nix-build --expr 'derivation {name = "test"; builder = "/bin/bash"; system = "x86_64-darwin";}'
this derivation will be built:
  /nix/store/i7fn0rj86xdx7mi49wsn4k79ncx89abg-test.drv
building '/nix/store/i7fn0rj86xdx7mi49wsn4k79ncx89abg-test.drv'...
error: executing '/bin/bash': Bad file descriptor
error: builder for '/nix/store/i7fn0rj86xdx7mi49wsn4k79ncx89abg-test.drv' failed with exit code 1
       note: build failure may have been caused by lack of free disk space

Can Nix tell me more? It can the --debug flag!

...
executing builder '/bin/bash'
using builder args ''
sandbox setup: Generated sandbox profile:
sandbox setup: (version 1)
sandbox setup:
sandbox setup:
sandbox setup: (allow default)
sandbox setup:
sandbox setup: ; Disallow creating setuid/setgid binaries, since that
sandbox setup: ; would allow breaking build user isolation.
sandbox setup: (deny file-write-setugid)
sandbox setup:
sandbox setup:
...

Can we disable the sandbox? I thought that if it's running in single-user mode, Nix won't use the sandbox. I need more information. Let's prepend xtrace to the nix-build command!

And now I have three million lines of debug output. Maybe that was a tad too much information, but I scrolled through it until I found what I wanted:

... lots of syscalls ...
 would allow breaking build user isolation. |(deny file-write-setugid)||| -> 159
 [1927] open("/private/tmp/nix-build-test.drv-0/.sandbox.sb", O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC|O_CLOEXEC, 438) -> 3
 [1927] write(3, 0x7FD660423F80, 158) -> 158|[1927] close(3) -> 0|[1927] lstat64("/private", 0x7FFFFFDFD1F8) -> 0
 [1927] lstat64("/private/tmp", 0x7FFFFFDFD1F8) -> 0
 [1927] write(2, 0x7FFFFFDFD4D1, 2)| -> 2
 [1927] posix_spawn(NULL, "/usr/bin/sandbox-exec",
        { attributes = POSIX_SPAWN_SETEXEC, file_actions = {} },
        {"sandbox-exec", "-f", "/private/tmp/nix-build-test.drv-0/.sandbox.sb",
        "-D", "_GLOBAL_TMP_DIR=/private/tmp", "/bin/bash"},
        {"HOME=/homeless-shelter", "NIX_BUILD_CORES=12",
        "NIX_BUILD_TOP=/private/tmp/nix-build-test.drv-0",
        "NIX_LOG_FD=2", "NIX_STORE=/nix/store", "PATH=/path-not-set",
        "PWD=/private/tmp/nix-build-test.drv-0", "TEMP=/private/tmp/nix-build-test.drv-0",
        "TEMPDIR=/private/tmp/nix-build-test.drv-0", "TERM=xterm-256color",
        "TMP=/private/tmp/nix-build-test.drv-0", "TMPDIR=/private/tmp/nix-build-test.drv-0",
        "builder=/bin/bash", "name=test", "out=/nix/store/1hc179ghyjlfy62f6wyis92yl814mhy7-test",
        "system=x86_64-darwin"}) -> ENOEXEC
 [1927] write_nocancel(2, 0x7FD660409D40, 82)error: executing '/bin/bash': Bad file descriptor -> 82
 [1927] exit(1))

It looks like sandbox-exec is being called, and it so happens that /usr/bin/sandbox-exec doesn't exist on Darling! Can we get around it? Where is sandbox-exec in the code?

if (getEnv("_NIX_TEST_NO_SANDBOX") != "1") {
    builder = "/usr/bin/sandbox-exec";
    args.push_back("sandbox-exec");
    args.push_back("-f");
    args.push_back(sandboxFile);
    args.push_back("-D");
    args.push_back("_GLOBAL_TMP_DIR=" + globalTmpDir);
    if (allowLocalNetworking) {
        args.push_back("-D");
        args.push_back(std::string("_ALLOW_LOCAL_NETWORKING=1"));
    }
    args.push_back(drv->builder);
} else {
    builder = drv->builder;
    args.push_back(std::string(baseNameOf(drv->builder)));
}

So if I set _NIX_TEST_NO_SANDBOX=1, then everything should work?

Darling# export _NIX_TEST_NO_SANDBOX=1
Darling# nix-shell '<home-manager>' -A install
...
mv: cannot move 'build.xml' to '/nix/store/svnf2aiagc98l1222ni7qycqa2i160r8-docbook-xsl-ns-1.79.2/share/xml/docbook-xsl-ns/build.xml': Function not implemented
Unimplemented syscall (488)
...

Another error. Wonderful. At least Nix is building things now!

In Which I Commit Nix Crimes™

It seems like the version of mv that Nix downloads is too new and is using a syscall that isn't supported by Darling. Let's replace that mv with our own!

Yes, modifying the contents of a Nix store directly is a bad idea. I just can't bring myself to care.

Darling# nix-shell '<home-manager>' -A install
this derivation will be built:
  /nix/store/424sw82b28ip4v59cyvxsx813lnkc7ic-options-docbook.xml.drv
building '/nix/store/424sw82b28ip4v59cyvxsx813lnkc7ic-options-docbook.xml.drv'...
/nix/store/j7lvji2dg1bkr7j8das3fak85hbxjzpx-python3-3.10.12/lib/python3.10/multiprocessing/resource_tracker.py:224: UserWarning: resource_tracker: There appear to be 6 leaked semaphore objects to clean up at shutdown
  warnings.warn('resource_tracker: There appear to be %d '
error: builder for '/nix/store/424sw82b28ip4v59cyvxsx813lnkc7ic-options-docbook.xml.drv' failed due to signal 15 (Terminated: 15)

Is the Python stuff causing an error? Peeking at the code, it's just a warning message and does not cause any error.

Let's find the smallest command that causes the issue so debugging is a little faster (running the install command takes a while, like everything else in Darling). Poking around in the derivation file, I extracted the actual command that's being run, which worked fine!

So what's the issue? Can I just skip building the docs?

Darling# nix-env -i git
these 31 paths will be fetched (9.51 MiB download, 62.77 MiB unpacked):
  /nix/store/j0hpv9n6fbnj1qaz4vzvd0zq9m30ss5l-git-2.41.0
...
copying path '/nix/store/j0hpv9n6fbnj1qaz4vzvd0zq9m30ss5l-git-2.41.0' from 'https://cache.nixos.org'...
building '/nix/store/iyn6m28k5mlh9zf8ynnkvnrnpny1msjl-user-environment.drv'...
Darling# git clone https://github.com/nix-community/home-manager
Cloning into 'home-manager'...
...
Resolving deltas: 100% (21733/21733), done.

I edited modules/modules.nix and removed ./manual.nix as well as removed all mention of the docs from default.nix. That should do it!

Darling# nix-shell -A install
these 6 derivations will be built:
...
Activating onFilesChange
Activating setupLaunchAgents
Segmentation fault: 11 (core dumped)

The sporadic crashes return. If this doesn't work when I run it again, I'll be pretty sad.

Darling# nix-shell -A install
...
Activating setupLaunchAgents
warning: Nix search path entry '/nix/var/nix/profiles/per-user/root/channels/nixos' does not exist, ignoring

There are 61 unread and relevant news items.
Read them by running the command "home-manager news".

All done! The home-manager tool should now be installed and you can edit

    /Users/root/.config/home-manager/home.nix

to configure Home Manager. Run 'man home-configuration.nix' to
see all available options.

Victory is mine! Now I just need to get home-manager to use my configs. I cloned my nix repo, copied in the new home.nix that was generated for me, and added the imports:

imports = [
    ./nvim.nix
    ./shell.nix
    ./common.nix
];

I linked the new config, ran nix-shell -A install and prayed. I'm not superstitious, but if praying would help then I would do it.

Builder called die: Cannot wrap '/nix/store/bk8qrbhk1kqyk2nz9fdydmd8x0vzfrw7-ltex-ls-16.0.0/bin/ltex-ls' because it is not an executable file
Backtrace:
10 assertExecutable /nix/store/pvbas0mzgl5a61wnpwpjah6xq94lsha3-make-binary-wrapper-hook/nix-support/setup-hook
63 wrapProgramBinary /nix/store/pvbas0mzgl5a61wnpwpjah6xq94lsha3-make-binary-wrapper-hook/nix-support/setup-hook
58 wrapProgram /nix/store/pvbas0mzgl5a61wnpwpjah6xq94lsha3-make-binary-wrapper-hook/nix-support/setup-hook
1613 genericBuild /nix/store/b0cb7r617xlk00aqnq65xlajf9m1xw8n-stdenv-darwin/setup
6 main /nix/store/6xg259477c90a229xwmb53pdfkn6ig3g-default-builder.sh

error: builder for '/nix/store/ddb97cb467hrim7iqb8izyk7ah8ryfqd-ltex-ls-16.0.0.drv' failed with exit code 1
       note: build failure may have been caused by lack of free disk space

I give up on trying to make it perfect. I removed ltex-ls (and everything else that was broken) from my nvim config. It's not like I'm really gonna use this environment for Markdown stuff, right?

I rebuild. It crashes on typescript-language-server. I delete it. Something else is broken, I get rid of it too. Such is Nix on a platform that was never meant to have it.

I'm beginning to question how useful Nix on Darling is really gonna be.

...
building '/nix/store/c5n06d7jd1hmsykampx34gc7qj1yyjsz-neovim-0.9.1.drv'...
Generating remote plugin manifest
error: builder for '/nix/store/c5n06d7jd1hmsykampx34gc7qj1yyjsz-neovim-0.9.1.drv' failed due to signal 11 (Segmentation fault: 11)

Of course it couldn't be that easy. What am I going to do, delete nvim? At this point I'm seriously considering it.

Time to debug again, I guess. Let's open the derivation in a shell so we can step through the issues.

Darling# nix-shell /nix/store/c5n06d7jd1hmsykampx34gc7qj1yyjsz-neovim-0.9.1.drv
Darling-nix# source $stdenv/setup
Darling-nix# set -x
Darling-nix# genericBuild
...
Generating remote plugin manifest
++ touch /nix/store/b8cxllw65bmk8yyj2qhczkxsh6vsd94j-neovim-0.9.1/rplugin.vim
Segmentation fault: 11 (core dumped)
...

Not only was mv broken, but so is touch! I commit another Nix Crime™ and rebuild home-manager.

...
There are 61 unread and relevant news items.
Read them by running the command "home-manager news".

All done! The home-manager tool should now be installed and you can edit

    /Users/root/.config/home-manager/home.nix

to configure Home Manager. Run 'man home-configuration.nix' to
see all available options.

It worked! Does nvim work?

So what if nvim sometimes crashes when you quit the program and messes up the terminal? When I close Vim, my work is already saved and the terminal can be fixed with the reset command!

I'm just glad it works. I have my text editor managed with Nix inside of a macOS compatibility layer that was installed with Nix on NixOS!

As long as I don't touch anything else or let anything update, it should be fine, right?

So What Now?

Yet again have I taken upon a project that was much larger in scope than I realized (or rather, I expanded the scope). Who knows, maybe I'll wait a little while longer for Darling to get some more feature-parity before doing anything else with this.

Perhaps I'll try and contribute to the Darling project and get everything to work. Maybe I'll forget all about Darling in a week and go onto another crazy project that may or may not involve Nix.

Besides, if I really need a Darwin environment to do Nix in, I'll just put together a Hackintosh.
